Proxima Centauri bhttps://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Artist’s_impression_of_Proxima_Centauri_b_shown_hypothetically_as_an_arid_rocky_super-earth.jpgArtist's conception of Proxima Centauri b as a terrestrial exoplanet, with Proxima Centauri and the [Alpha Centauri](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alpha_Centauri) system visible in the background. The actual appearance and composition of the exoplanet beyond this data is currently unknown.

Star[Proxima Centauri](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Proxima_Centauri)
Physical characteristics
[Mean radius](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mean_radius_(astronomy))

0.94–1.4 [R🜨](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Earth_radius)[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBruggerMousisDeleuilLunine20161-6)[[b]](#cite_note-7)
[Mass](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mass)≥1.055±0.055 [M🜨](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Earth_mass)[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESuárez_MascareñoArtigauMignonDelfosse202514-2)
[Temperature](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Temperature)[Teq](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Planetary_equilibrium_temperature): 234 K (−39 °C; −38 °F)[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAnglada-EscudéAmadoBarnesBerdiñas2016438-8)

Proxima Centauri b is an [exoplanet](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Exoplanet) orbiting within the [habitable zone](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Habitable_zone) of the [red dwarf](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Red_dwarf) star [Proxima Centauri](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Proxima_Centauri) in the [constellation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Constellation) [Centaurus](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Centaurus). It can also be referred to as Proxima b,[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETurbetLeconteSelsisBolmont20161-9) or Alpha Centauri Cb. The host star is the closest star to the [Sun](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sun), at a distance of about 4.2 [light-years](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Light-year) (1.3 [parsecs](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Parsec)) from [Earth](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Earth), and is part of the larger triple [star system](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Star_system) [Alpha Centauri](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alpha_Centauri). Proxima b and [Proxima d](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Proxima_Centauri_d), along with the currently disputed [Proxima c](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Proxima_Centauri_c), are the [closest known exoplanets](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_nearest_exoplanets) to the [Solar System](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_System).

Proxima Centauri b orbits its parent star at a distance of about 0.04848 AU (7.253 million km; 4.506 million mi) with an [orbital period](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Orbital_period) of approximately 11.2 Earth days. Its other properties are only poorly understood, but it is probably a terrestrial planet with a [minimum mass](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Minimum_mass) of 1.06 [M🜨](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Earth_mass) and a slightly larger radius than that of Earth. The planet orbits within the habitable zone of its parent star; but it is not known whether it has an atmosphere, which would impact the habitability probabilities. Proxima Centauri is a [flare star](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Flare_star) with intense emission of [electromagnetic radiation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Electromagnetic_radiation) that could strip an atmosphere off the planet.

Announced on 24 August 2016 by the [European Southern Observatory](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/European_Southern_Observatory) (ESO), Proxima Centauri b was confirmed via several years of [Doppler spectroscopy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Doppler_spectroscopy) measurements of its parent star. The detection of Proxima Centauri b was a major discovery in [planetology](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Planetology),[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMascareñoFariaFigueiraLovis20201-10) and has drawn interest to the Alpha Centauri star system as a whole.[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEQuarlesLissauer20181-11) As of 2023, Proxima Centauri b is believed to be the best-known exoplanet to the general public.[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMieliValliMaccone2023435-12) The exoplanet's proximity to Earth offers an opportunity for robotic space exploration.

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:The_motion_of_Proxima_Centauri_in_2016,_revealing_the_fingerprints_of_a_planet.jpgVelocity of Proxima Centauri towards and away from the Earth as measured with the HARPS spectrograph during the first three months of 2016. The red symbols with black error bars represent data points, and the blue curve is a fit of the data. The amplitude and period of the motion were used to estimate the planet's minimum mass.
Proxima Centauri had become a target for exoplanet searches already before the discovery of Proxima Centauri b, but initial studies in 2008 and 2009 ruled out the existence of larger-than-Earth exoplanets in the habitable zone.[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKippingCameronHartmanDavenport20171-13) Planets are very common around dwarf stars, with on average 1–2 planets per star,[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKippingCameronHartmanDavenport20172-14) and about 20–40% of all red dwarfs have one in the habitable zone.[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWandel2017498-15) Additionally, red dwarfs are by far the most common type of stars.[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMeadowsArneySchwietermanLustig-Yaeger2018133-16)

Based on observations with instruments[[c]](#cite_note-18) at the [European Southern Observatory](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/European_Southern_Observatory) in Chile prior to 2016, motion anomalies were identified in Proxima Centauri[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAnglada-EscudéAmadoBarnesBerdiñas2016437-17) that could not be satisfactorily explained by [flares](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Flare)[[d]](#cite_note-20) or [chromospheric](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chromosphere)[[e]](#cite_note-22) activity of the star. This suggested that Proxima Centauri may be orbited by an exoplanet. In January 2016, a team of astronomers launched the Pale Red Dot project to confirm this hypothetical exoplanet's existence. On 24 August 2016, the team led by [Anglada-Escudé](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Guillem_Anglada-Escudé) proposed that a [terrestrial exoplanet](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Terrestrial_exoplanet) in the habitable zone of Proxima Centauri could explain these anomalies and announced Proxima Centauri b's discovery.[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAnglada-EscudéAmadoBarnesBerdiñas2016438-8)

In 2022, the exoplanet [Proxima Centauri d](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Proxima_Centauri_d), which orbits even closer to the star, was confirmed.[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFariaMascareñoFigueiraSilva202210-23) An exoplanet candidate named [Proxima Centauri c](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Proxima_Centauri_c) was reported in 2020,[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESirajLoeb20201-24) but its existence has since been disputed due to potential artifacts in the data.[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEArtigauCadieuxCookDoyon20221-25) The claimed existence of a [dust](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dust) belt around Proxima Centauri remains unconfirmed.[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENoackKislyakovaJohnstoneGüdel20211-26)

## Physical properties
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Proxima_Centauri_b&action=edit&section=2)]

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Proxima_Centauri_and_its_planet_compared_to_the_Solar_System.jpgOverview and comparison of the orbital distance of the habitable zones of Proxima Centauri compared to the [Solar System](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_System)

### Distance, orbital parameters and age
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Proxima_Centauri_b&action=edit&section=3)]

Proxima Centauri b is the closest exoplanet to Earth,[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchulze-MakuchIrwin2018240-27) at a distance of about 4.2 [ly](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Light-year) (1.3 [parsecs](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Parsec)).[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETurbetLeconteSelsisBolmont20161-9) It orbits Proxima Centauri every 11.185 Earth days at a distance of about 0.048 [AU](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Astronomical_unit),[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESuárez_MascareñoArtigauMignonDelfosse202514-2) over 20 times closer to Proxima Centauri than Earth is to the Sun.[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGarraffoDrakeCohen20161-28) As of 2021, it is unclear whether it has a significant [eccentricity](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Orbital_eccentricity)[[f]](#cite_note-31)[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENoackKislyakovaJohnstoneGüdel20219-32) but Proxima Centauri b is unlikely to have any [obliquity](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Obliquity).[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGarraffoDrakeCohen20162-33) The age of the planet is unknown;[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERitchieLarkumRibas2018148-34) Proxima Centauri itself may have been captured by [Alpha Centauri](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alpha_Centauri) and thus not necessarily of the same age as the latter pair of stars, which are about 5 billion years old.[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENoackKislyakovaJohnstoneGüdel20211-26) Proxima Centauri b is unlikely to have stable orbits for [moons](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Natural_satellite).[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKreidbergLoeb20162-35)

### Mass, radius and composition
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Proxima_Centauri_b&action=edit&section=4)]

As of 2025, the estimated minimum mass of Proxima Centauri b is 1.055±0.055 [M🜨](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Earth_mass);[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESuárez_MascareñoArtigauMignonDelfosse202514-2) other estimates are similar,[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMascareñoFariaFigueiraLovis20207-36) but all estimates are a minimum because the [inclination](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Inclination) of the planet's orbit is not yet known.[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENoackKislyakovaJohnstoneGüdel20211-26) Assuming an inclination of 47°, coplanar with its host star's rotation, its true mass would be 1.44±0.21 [M🜨](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Earth_mass).[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESuárez_MascareñoArtigauMignonDelfosse202514-2) This makes it similar to [Earth](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Earth), but the radius of the planet is poorly known and hard to determine—estimates based on possible composition give a range of 0.94 to 1.4 [R🜨](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Earth_radius),[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBruggerMousisDeleuilLunine20161-6)[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDe_LucaBraamKomacekHochman20241481-37) and its mass may border on the cutoff between Earth-type and [Neptune](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Neptune)-type planets, if that value is lower than previously estimated.[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKippingCameronHartmanDavenport20172-14) Depending on the composition, Proxima Centauri b could range from being a [Mercury](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mercury_(planet))-like planet with a large [core](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Core_(geology))—which would require particular conditions early in the planet's history—to a very water-rich planet. Observations of the [Fe](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Iron)–[Si](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Silicon)–[Mg](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Magnesium) ratios of Proxima Centauri may allow a determination of the composition of the planet,[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBruggerMousisDeleuilLunine20164-38) since they are expected to roughly match the ratios of any planetary bodies in the Proxima Centauri system; various observations have found Solar System-like ratios of these elements.[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENoackKislyakovaJohnstoneGüdel20212-39)

Little is known about Proxima Centauri b as of 2021—mainly its distance from the star and its orbital period[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGaluzzoCagnazzoBerrilliFierli20211-40)—but a number of [simulations](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Simulation) of its physical properties have been done.[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENoackKislyakovaJohnstoneGüdel20211-26) A number of simulations and models have been created that assume Earth-like compositions[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZuluagaBustamante201855-41) and include predictions of the [galactic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Galaxy) environment, [internal heat](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Internal_heat) generation from [radioactive decay](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radioactive_decay) and [magnetic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Magnetic) [induction heating](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Induction_heating),[[g]](#cite_note-44) planetary rotation, the effects of stellar radiation, the amount of [volatile](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Volatile_(astrogeology)) elements the planet consists of and the changes of these parameters over time.[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENoackKislyakovaJohnstoneGüdel20212-39)

Proxima Centauri b likely developed under different conditions from Earth, with less water, stronger [impacts](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Impact_event) and an overall faster development, assuming that it formed at its current distance from the star.[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERibasBolmontSelsisReiners20163-45) Proxima Centauri b probably did not form at its current distance to Proxima Centauri, as the amount of material in the [protoplanetary disk](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Protoplanetary_disk) would be insufficient. Instead, the planet, or protoplanetary fragments, likely formed at larger distances and then migrated to the current orbit of Proxima Centauri b. Depending on the nature of the precursor material, it may be rich in volatiles.[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAnglada-EscudéAmadoBarnesBerdiñas2016438-8) A number of different formation scenarios are possible, many of which depend on the existence of other planets around Proxima Centauri and which would result in different compositions.[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEColemanNelsonPaardekooperDreizler20171007-46)

Proxima Centauri b is likely to be [tidally locked](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tidally_locked) to the host star,[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKreidbergLoeb20162-35) which for a 1:1 orbit would mean that the same side of the planet would always face Proxima Centauri.[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERitchieLarkumRibas2018148-34) It is unclear whether habitable conditions can arise under such circumstances[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESnellenDésertWatersRobinson20172-47) as a 1:1 tidal lock would lead to an extreme climate with only part of the planet habitable.[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERitchieLarkumRibas2018148-34)

However, the planet may not be tidally locked. If the eccentricity of Proxima Centauri b was higher than 0.1[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWalterováBěhounková202018-48)–0.06, it would tend to enter a Mercury-like 3:2 resonance[[h]](#cite_note-49) or higher-order resonances such as 2:1.[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETurbetLeconteSelsisBolmont20162-50) Additional planets around Proxima Centauri and interactions[[i]](#cite_note-51) with [Alpha Centauri](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alpha_Centauri) could excite higher eccentricies.[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMeadowsArneySchwietermanLustig-Yaeger2018138-52) If the planet is not symmetrical ([triaxial](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Triaxial_ellipsoid)), a capture into a non-tidally locked orbit would be possible even with low eccentricity.[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERibasBolmontSelsisReiners201610-53) A non-locked orbit, however, would result in [tidal heating](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tidal_heating) of the planet's [mantle](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mantle_(geology)), increasing [volcanic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Volcanic) activity and potentially shutting down a magnetic field-generating [dynamo](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dynamo_theory).[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMeadowsArneySchwietermanLustig-Yaeger2018136-54) The exact dynamics are strongly dependent on the internal structure of the planet and its evolution in response to tidal heating.[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWalterováBěhounková202022-55) A non-locked planet may experience [ocean tides](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ocean_tide) much more intense than Earth.[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShiYangAbbotLiu20254-56)

In May 2026, a study of Proxima Centauri found evidence of star-planet magnetic interaction for both [Proxima Centauri d](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Proxima_Centauri_d) and Proxima Centauri b, implying the likely presence of [magnetic fields](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Magnetosphere) on both planets. Similar star-planet interaction was previously observed at [YZ Ceti b](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/YZ_Ceti_b).[[48]](#cite_note-ZapateroOsorio2026-57)

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Angular_apparent_size_comparison.tifAn angular size comparison of how Proxima will appear in the sky seen from Proxima b (96'), compared with how the Sun appears in our sky on Earth (32'). Proxima is much smaller than the Sun, but Proxima b is very close to its star.

Proxima Centauri b's parent star Proxima Centauri is a [red dwarf](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Red_dwarf),[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETurbetLeconteSelsisBolmont20162-50) radiating only 0.005% of the amount of visible light that the Sun does and an average of about 0.17% of the Sun's energy.[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESiegel2016-58) Despite this low radiation, due to its close orbit Proxima Centauri b still receives about 70% of the amount of infrared energy that the Earth receives from the Sun.[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESiegel2016-58) Proxima Centauri is also a [flare star](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Flare_star) with its luminosity at times varying by a factor of 100 over a timespan of hours,[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERibasBolmontSelsisReiners20164-59) its luminosity averaged at 0.155±0.006 [L☉](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_luminosity).[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAnglada-EscudéAmadoBarnesBerdiñas2016438-8)

Proxima Centauri has 12.2% of the [Sun's mass](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_mass) and 15.4% of the [radius of the Sun](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_radius).[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKervellaThéveninLovis20173-60) With an [effective temperature](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Effectiv

Based on the Wikipedia article I fetched, **Proxima Centauri b** has an **orbital period of approximately 11.2 Earth days**.

More precisely, the latest data shows:
- **Orbital period: 11.18465 ± 0.00053 days** (about 11.2 days)

This short orbital period is due to the planet's close proximity to its host star—orbiting at just 0.048 AU (about 7.25 million km), which is over 20 times closer to Proxima Centauri than Earth is to the Sun.
